coredata.dc:title: Perspectives of magnetic nature carbon dots in analytical chemistry: From separation to detection and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Koduru, Janardhan Reddy
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic nature carbon dots (MNCDs) are fast growing materials with extremely unique physico-chemical properties and physiological ability to extend their applications from separation science to detection and bio-/magnetic resonance imaging applications. Recent studies have revealed that the MNCDs are significantly used as promising agents in analytical chemistry for the separation and identification of trace level target analytes. Further, the MNCDs have been used as probes for bioimaging of cells and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of tumors. Due to the lack of comprehensive reviews in this emerging field especially MNCDs applications in analytical chemistry, this review may provide quick guide and reference on the MNCDs-based analytical approaches for the separation and detection of trace level analytes, and bio- and MR- imaging of various cells. In this review article, we will summarize the synthetic approaches for the fabrication of MNCDs. The main part of this proposed review is devoted to the tremendous applications of MNCDs (Fe3O4@CDs, metal ion (Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Gd2+)-doped CDs, MnO2@CDs) in analytical chemistry from separation science to detection and bio- and MR imaging. Finally, we will explore the challenges and future prospects of magneto fluorescent carbon dots in biomedical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic nature carbon dots (MNCDs) are fast growing materials with extremely unique physico-chemical properties and physiological ability to extend their applications from separation science to detection and bio-/magnetic resonance imaging applications. Recent studies have revealed that the MNCDs are significantly used as promising agents in analytical chemistry for the separation and identification of trace level target analytes. Further, the MNCDs have been used as probes for bioimaging of cells and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of tumors. Due to the lack of comprehensive reviews in this emerging field especially MNCDs applications in analytical chemistry, this review may provide quick guide and reference on the MNCDs-based analytical approaches for the separation and detection of trace level analytes, and bio- and MR- imaging of various cells. In this review article, we will summarize the synthetic approaches for the fabrication of MNCDs. The main part of this proposed review is devoted to the tremendous applications of MNCDs (Fe3O4@CDs, metal ion (Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Gd2+)-doped CDs, MnO2@CDs) in analytical chemistry from separation science to detection and bio- and MR imaging. Finally, we will explore the challenges and future prospects of magneto fluorescent carbon dots in biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, magnetic nature carbon dots (MNCDs) have explored as a new class of green fluorescent nanomaterials and shown outstanding applications in sensing and biomedical applications [1–3]. The design and fabrication of next-generation biomaterials in the development of biomedical tools is gaining steam for biomedical and molecular biology research [2]. The pure carbon dots (CDs) and pure magnetic nanoparticles may not offer for multimodal imaging applications [4–7]. Recently, magneto fluorescent carbon dots play important roles in modern biomaterials, especially as drug delivery, bioimaging and magnetic resonance imaging agents in molecular biology and biomedical fields [8]. The interesting features of magneto fluorescent carbon dots are mainly attributed to the diverse surface chemistry on carbon dots and magnetic nature due to the presence of magnetic elements or nanocomposites [9]. Because of magnetic nature, magneto carbon dots have also been applied as probes for various applications including sensing, bioimaging, cell separation, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents, cancer treatment and drug delivery. Thus, this review provides significant information on the developments of magneto fluorescent carbon dots and their applications in analytical chemistry (separation, sensing and bioimaging) and biomedical fields.

In recent years, the photocatalysis, optoelectronics, sensing and bioimaging applications of carbon dots have already been reviewed in the literature [S1-S2, “S” represents the reference in Supporting Information]. So far, several reviews have been well explained about the synthetic strategies, characterization and properties of carbon dots [S2-S9, 10–13]. For example, Melvin Ng and co-workers reviewed about the hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods for the fabrication of CDs and their applications in heavy metal ions sensing [10–13]. Andrés’s team overviewed the properties and the basic synthetic routes and their applications in separation science [S4]. Nazri et al. reviewed the optical sensing applications of CDs [S5]. Wang’s well summarized the preparation methods for CDs and their biomedical applications [S6]. Chen et al. explored the outstanding applications of CDs in separation science and sample preparations [S7]. Alaghmandfard’s group demonstrated the surface modifications of CDs with different ligands and their biological applications [S8]. Shen et al. overviewed the synthesis, properties and applications of chemiluminescent CDs [S9]. Tejwan and co-workers well explained about the doping of metal ion into CDs and hybrid carbon dots synthesis and their applications [10]. Ansari et al. summarized the synthetic methods for the doping of heteroatoms into CDs and their biomedical applications (photothermal and photodynamic therapies, biosensing, in vitro and in vivo studies) [11]. Chen’s group overviewed the applications of CDs in imaging analysis of cancer cells [12]. Miao et al. extensively summarized the synthetic strategies, properties and applications of hetero-atom-doped CDs [13]. These reviews demonstrated that significant efforts have been dedicated on the synthetic routes, properties, surface functionalization and their analytical and biomedical applications. Noticeably, no review is summarized the magnetic nature carbon dots (MNCDs) and their analytical and biomedical applications.

This review intends to explore the fabrication of MNCDs and their analytical (separation and sensing) and imaging (bio and MR) applications, which provides quick reference about magnetic nature CDs. Particularly, we emphasis the MNCDs-based extraction strategies (solid-phase microexctraction (SPME), liquid microextraction, solid-phase extraction) for the separation and preconcentration of trace level chemical species from complex samples. This review also summarizes the applications of MNCDs in sensing of various analytes and bio- and MR- imaging of cells and tumors. Noticeably, the key applications of MNCDs as promising candidates in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents is discussed. In the end, we conclude the applications of MNCDs in analytical chemistry (sensing, bioimaging and separation science) and biomedical fields.

Recently, a wide variety of synthetic approaches has been illustrated for the fabrication of CDs with magnetic nature [11, S10-S13]. Briefly, solvothermal, reduction, hydrothermal, microwave, sonochemical, precipitation, electrochemical and carbonization methods have been used for the fabrication of MNCDs. It was observed that the incorporation of metal ions (Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Gd3+ etc) into CDs has significantly changed their non-magnetic to magnetic nature, thereby applying them as potential candidates in MRI and separation science. Similarly, magnetic nature was generated on carbon dots by mixing of CDs with magnetic nanocomposites (Fe2O3, MnO2 and Gd2O3), which results to form CDs hybrid nanostructured materials [10,14]. To generate magnetic nature CDs, magnetite NPs (Fe3O4) were efficiently functionalized with carbon nanostructures by solvothermal method using hydrogen peroxide and ferrocene as precursors [S14]. The as-prepared Fe3O4@C-Ag hybrid nanostructures are in dumbbell shape. Porous carbon shell (Fe3O4@CDs) nanostructured hybrid materials were successfully synthesized using ferrocene and H2O2 as precursor and acetone as a solvent via solvothermal method [S15-S16]. In these strategies, the cyclopentadiene undergoes decomposition that results to produce carbon free radicals, favouring to form ordered and disordered carbon nanostructures on the surfaces of Fe3O4 NPs. Later on, Au NPs were embedded within Fe3O4@CDs to form magnetic nature nanohybrid materials [S17]. Hydrothermal approaches were successfully demonstrated for the preparation of FeN@CDs [S18] and of CDs incorporated into hematite α-Fe2O3 using riboflavin (Rf), folic acid (FA) and genipin as reagents [S19].

Further, a wide variety of MNCDs namely MnO2@CDs [15], Fe3O4@CDs [16], Fe2O3@C [17], chlorine e6 (Ce6)-Gd3+ ion-CDs [18], Fe3O4@SiO2-CDs [19], cysteine-Ag@Fe3O4@CDs [20], aptamer-CDs@CoOOH [21], Fe2O3@graphene quantum dots (GQD)@CDs [22], Ga@CDs [23] and Gd3+ ion@CDs [24–26] have been synthesized and studied their analytical applications and biocompatible properties. These synthetic strategies demonstrated that the CDs were efficiently changed to magnetic nature by either doping magnetic metal ions or incorporating CDs on the surfaces of magnetic nanostructure materials. The MNCDs are formed by very simple and straight forward procedures within minutes to hours. Noticeably, the doping/functionalization of magnetic metal ions/composites play key roles in tuning their optical, morphology, physico-chemical and electrochemical properties, favouring them to use as outstanding materials in analytical chemistry. Fig. 1 demonstrates the synthetic routes for the fabrication of MNCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic and optical properties of MNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Many synthetic approaches have been adopted to fabricate magnetic nature carbon dots such as hydrothermal, microwave, sonochemical and hot plate. Magnetic properties of carbon dots are mainly dependent on the doping of magnetic metal ions or metal oxides [27,28]. It was observed that the magnetic properties of carbon dots can be tuned by their compositions (doping of magnetic metal ions or fabrication of magnetic hybrid carbon dots). As a result, the incorporation of metal ions (Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Gd3+ etc) into CDs or the fabrication of magnetic nature nanocomposites (Fe2O3 and MnO2) hybrid CDs lead to generate magnetic properties (ferromagnetic, paramagnetic and superparamagnetic), resulting to generate hybrid nanostructure materials, which allows to use them in wide variety applications including separation, sensing and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging [10–13]. Experimentally, it was proved that the magnetization values of CDs can be remarkably improved by doping magnetic nature elements (Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Gd3+ etc) into CDs nanostructures [28]. Further, Fe has been considered as one of the ferromagnetic material that has mass magnetization value of ∼220 emu g–1, suggesting the magnetic nature of CDs could be greatly enhanced by preparing Fe-based carbon nanostructure hybrid materials [14]. Importantly, iron oxides (cubic and hexagonal ferrites) exhibited magnetic nature with acceptable values and high chemical stability, favoring them to use as promising metal oxide nanostructures to functionalize with CDs [29]. It was also observed that the N,Co-doped CDs exhibited ferromagnetic nature (saturated magnetization = 1.476 emu/g) due to the presence of unpaired electrons in Co2+ ion-doped CDs [28]. Authors observed that N,Co-doped CDs exhibited smaller saturated magnetization value as compared to other metal ion-doped CDs and hybrid CDs nanocomposites. In literature, several paramagnetic and super-paramagnetic CDs and hybrid CDs nanocomposites have been recognized as potential candidates for MR imaging applications, however, N, Co-doped CDs nanostructures acted as promising agents for extracting of pathogenic microbial species and cancer cells from complex matrices due to their ferromagnetic nature [28]. These results illustrated that the magnetic nature carbon dots will be generated by either doping of metal ions or preparing hybrid carbon dots.

Generally, the fluorescence properties of CDs nanostructures can be tuned by various synthetic routes, conditions (solvents, temperature and pH) and precursors [11]. Noticeably, the photoluminescence properties of CDs are strongly influenced by carbon core, surface state, molecular state and dopants. Spectral data of magnetic CDs nanostructures revealed that the metal ion doped CDs and magnetic hybrid CDs exhibited the UV–vis absorption in the range of 240–360 nm due to the π → π* and n → π* electronic transitions, suggesting the presence of sp2 carbon and active functional groups (COOH, CO, CN, CHO and OH) [
10–14,28]. Further, optical properties of CDs can be tuned by incorporating heteroatoms (S, N, P, B and F), favouring to generate new nanostructure CDs with unique optical and electronic properties, which extends their applications in multidisciplinary science [29]. Apart from these non-metals, metal ions (Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Gd3+ etc) were successfully used as dopants for generation of magnetic nature carbon dots and exhibited good optical, electronic and magnetic properties [29]. Similarly, carbon dots were functionalized onto the surface of metal oxide nanoparticles (Fe2O3 and MnO2), displaying good optical properties for the identification of various target molecules. Further, the magnetic nature carbon nanostructures of emission peaks were noticed in the range of 430–650 nm, signifying the doping of magnetic nature metal ions did not much influence the optical properties of CDs. Based on the summary on the synthetic approaches and optical properties of magnetic nature carbon nanostructures, the as-fabricated magnetic nature CDs nanostructures exhibited good fluorescence properties, acceptable quantum yields and lifetimes, exploring them as potential candidates for sensing and bioimaging applications.

Properties of materials are mainly determined by their structures, while the applications are largely determined by their properties. Thus, the MNCDs are advantageous for many applications including sensing, bioimaging, separation and biomedical, because of their specific properties. Surface functionalization of MNCDs with various ligands (e.g., organic molecules/inorganic composites, metal ions, polymers, biomolecules (nucleic acids, proteins, peptides and aminoacids)) is an effective pathway for tuning the properties of MNCDs. As a result, MNCDs have been successfully integrated with various sample preparation tools and separation techniques for the separation of trace level analytes from the complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic nature carbon dots in separation science
ce:section.ce:para: In the recent years, separation science has been significantly improved by the integration of wide variety nanomaterials as separation media [30–34]. Recently, carbon nanocomposites have been used as promising materials for the extraction and separation of chemical species from various sample matrices [14]. The MNCDs have been actively used as potential candidates in separation science (miniaturized extraction tools and chromatography). For example, oxycodone was successfully extracted and then analyzed by using molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) - magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) and CDs nanocomposites as adsorbents via ultrasonic-assisted dispersive solid-phase microextraction (UA-DSPME) coupled with high performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC) technique [14]. Langmuir adsorption isotherms demonstrated that the maximum adsorption capacity was noticed using 99 mg/g of MNCDs, exhibiting impressive limit of detection (LOD) of 0.80 ng/mL, which has ability to detect oxycodone in human urine. To demonstrate the specific application of mannitol-Fe3O4 NPs and B-doped CDs in analytical chemistry (separation and sensing), mannitol-Fe3O4 NPs were used as adsorbents in dispersive solid-phase microextraction (DSPME) of drugs (orbifloxacin (ORX) and sparfloxacin (SPX)) from water and milk samples [35]. The extraction of drugs was based on the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. The detection of both drugs was based on the enhancement of fluorescence intesnity via the formation of electron-donor-acceptor complex and H-bonding between B-doped CDs and drugs. This method exhibited good linear ranges 1.2 × 10−9 - 1.1 × 10−7 and 1.5 × 10−9 - 1.3 × 10−7 M with LODs of 4.0 × 10−10 and 5.0 × 10−10 for ORX and SPX, respectively. The developed method was compared with the other analytical techniques, exhibiting higher sensitivity than that of reported methods, which could be utilized for the detection of SPX and ORX in water and milk samples with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Similarly, Fe3O4-oleic acid (OA)-CDs were efficiently used as a ferrofluid for the extraction and identification of phenolic compounds (bisphenol-AF (BPAF), bisphenol-A (BPA), 4-tert-octylphenol (4-TOP) and tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA)) from pretreated milk and water samples by DSPME-HPLC [36]. To prepare ferrofluid, Fe3O4-OA@CDs were dispersed in ionic liquids (ILs), showing good dispersion, which was greatly improved the extraction efficiency. Importantly, authors evaluated the role of CDs in DSPME by preparing two types of ferrofluids i.e., ferrofluid-I (Fe3O4-OA) and ferrofluid-II (Fe3O4-OA@CDs) dispersed in IL of [C8MIm]PF6. The developed method has ability to extract phenolic compounds from milk using ILs- Fe3O4-OA@CDs (Fig. 2 a), and successfully applied to detect phenolic compounds in milk by HPLC (Fig. 2b). It was noticed that ferrofluid-II exhibited superior extraction efficiency than that of ferrofluid-I, suggesting the functionalization of CDs on the surfaces of Fe3O4-OA was greatly improved the extraction efficiency of DSPME, which allows to achieve the lower LODs in the range of 0.09−0.17 μg L−1. Among several ILs, [C8MIm]PF6 was found to be the best carrier to prepare ferrofluid for the DSPME of phenolic compounds. A facile chemical method was established for the preparation of Fe3O4@N-doped CDs for preconcentration and extraction of Pb2+ ion from water and vegetable samples [37]. The as-prepared Fe3O4@N-doped CDs were successfully used as sorbents for the extraction of Pb2+ ion from complex samples and then analyzed by flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS). This method exhibited the LOD of 0.082 μg L−1. Magnetic field enhanced SPME was developed for the extraction of ascorbic acid and then analyzed by a bimodal electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method [38]. In this approach, Fe3O4@chitosan@graphene oxide nanocomposite was used as sorbent for the extraction of ascorbic acid and then analyzed by ECL via Tris(2,2′-bipyridine)dichlororuthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+)/-CDs/ascorbic acid system where ECL intensity was enhanced with increasing concentration of ascorbic acid. Authors obtained an impressive LOD of 330 pM and successfully applied to detect ascorbic acid in orangeade.

Graphene-like MNCDs nanoflowers (MoS2@Fe3O4@CDs) were successfully fabricated by ecofriendly hydrothermal method [39]. The fabricated MNCDs nanoflowers were efficiently used as adsorbents for the SMPE of ibuprofen from various samples. The extraction mechanism was based on the H-bonding between ibuprofen and MNCDs nanoflowers. Authors noticed that the efficient extraction and preconcentration of ibuprofen from the complex samples prior to its quantification by HPLC. This method showed good recovery ranges from 89 to 105 % with lower detection limit of 11 × 10−6 μg mL−1 and preconcentration factor of 100, respectively. Similarly, zero valent Fe-rGO nanocomposites were introduced as a new sorbent for SPME of various organophosphorus pesticides (diazinon, parathion, malathion, sevin and fenitrothion) [40]. The best SPME was noticed using 15 mg of sorbent, pH 7.0, sample volume 10.0 mL, and extraction time 5.0 min, respectively. The extracted pesticides were successfully analyzed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS). This method showed lower LODs <0.07 ng mL−1 for five pesticides, and successfully applied to detect five pesticides in fruits (apple and grape) and water samples with good recoveries. The MNCDs namely CDs@poly(glycidyl methacrylate)@Fe3O4 NPs have been integrated with microwave-assisted SPME coupled with HPLC for the analysis of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) (benzopyrene, perylene, and 9,10-diphenylanthracene) in environmental samples [41]. Authors investigated several key parameters including microwave energy, adsorption/desorption time, and solvents for efficient SPME of PAHs. This method exhibited good linear ranges from 0.02–200 μg L−1, with lower LODs in the ranges of 20–90 ng L-1 for PAHs. The developed method exhibited good precision and accuracy for the analysis of PAHs in environmental water samples.

To extend the applications of MNCDs, Fe3O4/N-CDs have been used as sorbents for the extraction of Cd2+ ion in mollusk and fish samples [42]. The extracted Cd2+ ion was efficiently quantified by FAAS. It was noticed that pH (6.30) extraction time (5.0 min) adsorbent amount (10 mg) and desorption solvent (1.5 mL) and desorption solvent concentration (0.5 M) were found to be the optimum conditions for the extraction of Cd2+ ion. The developed method showed impressive LOD of 0.06 μg L−1, and successfully applied to detect Cd2+ ion in mollusk and fish samples. The combination of Fe3O4-hexanoic acid-SPME with N-doped CDs was explored as a promising analytical method for the selective and sensitive detection of FA in serum samples [43]. In this approach, Fe3O4-hexanoic acid NPs were prepared by one-pot co-precipitation method and used as sorbents for the SPME of FA. The Fe3O4-hexanoic acid NPs were efficiently extracted FA from human serum sample (Fig. 3 a). The Fe3O4-hexanoic acid NPs-FA conjugates were dispersed in N-doped CDs solutions for the identification of FA by fluorescence spectrometry (Fig. 3b-c). The efficient extraction and purification were achieved using 100 μL of Fe3O4-hexanoic acid NPs. The fluorescence assay of FA was based on the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs at 440 nm upon the addition of FA. As a result, this method exhibited good linear range (0.01–30 μM) with the LOD of 0.5 nM. The developed Fe3O4-hexanoic acid-SPME with N-doped CDs-based fluorescence method was successfully utilized for assaying of FA in serum samples. Further, CDs were functionalized on the surfaces of Fe3O4 NPs and then integrated with SPME for parallel adsorption and monitoring of U6+ ion in polluted mining water [44]. In this approach, super-paramagnetic Fe3O4 NPs (∼8.0 nm) were prepared by simple precipitation method and CDs were also prepared by facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach. Then, CDs were conjugated with Fe3O4 NPs using 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide as a reagent under reflux at 60 °C. The maximum adsorption efficiency of U6+ ion was noticed in the pH range of 4.3–4.8, revealing the removal of 99 % U6+ ion from water within 30 min–1 h. The fluorescence assay of U6+ ion was based on the quenching of emission peak at 490 nm with increasing concentration of U6+ ion, exhibiting impressive LOD at 0.8 ppm. Importantly, authors noticed that the strong interference from Fe3+ ion during the identification of U6+ ion, however it was removed by the precipitation method. The developed method exhibited good recovery ranges and found to be an ecofriendly approach for the removal and analysis of U6+ ion in water samples.

Fe3O4/hydroxyapatite@graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite was efficiently integrated with ultrasound-assisted SPME of Cu2+ ion from Thai food ingredients prior to the detection of Cu2+ ion by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [S20]. Authors noticed that the best SPME of Cu2+ ion was achieved using pH 5.0, magnetic nanocomposites (0.02 g), adsorption time (10 min), eluting agent (5.0 mL of 1.0 M HNO3) as optimum conditions. Under the optimal parameters, this method exhibited wider linear range from 0.05–1500 ng mL−1 with the LOD of 0.58 ng mL−1, which showed ability to the lower limit of quantification (LOQ) 1.94 ng mL−1. This method showed good recovery in the range of 83.5–104.8%, demonstrating the precision and accuracy of the method. The designed magnetic nanocomposites exhibited good selectivity for SPME of Cu2+ ion in the presence of several metal ions (Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, As3+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Fe3+. Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions. Similarly, Fe3O4@GQDs were introduced as a novel sorbents for solid phase extraction (SPE) of bisphenol A in water samples prior to its analysis by HPLC [S21]. The developed method showed wider linear range (0.1–300 ng mL−1) with impressive a LOD of 12.3 pg mL−1 and enrichment factor of 360. The developed Fe3O4@GQDs-SPE-HPLC method was utilized for the detection of bisphenol A in plastic water bottles after exposing to the sunlight. Similarly, nine pesticides (penconazole, oxadiazon, haloxyfop–R–methyl, chlorpyrifos, clodinafop–propargyl, fenazaquin, diniconazole, fenoxaprop–p–ethyl and fenpropathrin) were successfully extracted from different packed fruit juices (pomegranate, apricot, watermelon, sour cherry, grape, orange juices and apple) using a poly (ε–caprolactone) grafted-GQDs-assisted DSPE coupled with dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction [S22]. In this approach, the target analytes were efficiently adsorbed on the surfaces of modified GQDs and then target analytes were eluted using methanol as eluting solvent. Then, the eluant (μL) was mixed with 1,1,2,2-tetrachloroethane as a preconcentrating solvent (1,1,2–TCE) at μL–level and then analyzed by gas chromatography (GC). This method exhibited lower LODs of 0.32–0.76 ng mL–1 and the limit of quantifications (1.1–2.6 ng mL–1) with higher enrichment factors in the range of 680–840 and of 340–420 for nine pesticides. Further, GOQDs were integrated with SPE of three metal ions (Cd2+, As3+ and Pb2+) from complex samples prior their assay by photoluminescent array sensor [S23]. This method exhibited good recovery ranges (∼80 %) using metal ions concentrations from 10−2 to 102 μM, offering lower LODs of 41.1, 5.03 and 4.44 nM for Cd2+, As3+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively, which showed good recovery ranges from 83.52–128.3% for the above three metal ions from environmental samples. Another work demonstrated the remarkable application of Fe3O4 NPs-assisted SPE of nicotine from shredded tobacco and then quantified by using Cu2+ ion-N-doped CDs as a fluorescent probe [S24]. The Fe3O4 NPs acted as selective adsorbents for nicotine, thereby removing the other chemical substances in tobacco by the physical adsorption mechanism. Noticeably, the characteristic emission peaks of Cu2+ ion-N-doped CDs at 294 and 580 nm under a single excitation at 270 nm were remarkably quenched by the addition of nicotine. As a result, a dynamic linear range was noticed between the concentration of nicotine (0.1–10 μg mL−1) and emission intensity, offering lower LOD of 0.01 μg mL−1.

Apart from these progresses, several reports have been explored the advances of CDs with SiO2 nanocomposites as stationary phase in various chromatographic techniques (HPLC, GC, capillary electrophoresis) for the separation and identification of wide variety trace level analytes [S25-S31]. However, these reports are not discussed in this section since they are not dealing with the magnetic nature. Overall, the use of CDs-based nanocomposites as stationary phase exhibited efficient platform for the separation of trace level target analytes via complex retention mechanisms including adsorption, partitioning, adsorption interaction and the multiple retention interactions (π-π electron-donor-acceptor and π-π stacking interactions), which plays a key role in separating the target analytes. Table 1 summarizes the chemical composition, optical properties of MNCDs and their applications in separation science.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic nature carbon dots in sensing applications
ce:section.ce:para: In past two decades, the CDs have witnessed in exploring their outstanding applications in analytical chemistry because of their remarkable properties (fluorescence, biocompatibility, quantum yield, dispersability and surface chemistry) [45–51]. Since the optical properties of CDs were greatly improved by inserting Ln3+ ion and heteroatoms, thereby acting as promising probes for the sensing of trace level analytes and bioimaging of various cells [52–57] Recently, the MNCDs have been used as promising probes for sensing of wide variety target analytes from the sample matrices [26,28]. Noticeably, the MNCDs exhibited god quantum yield (QY), and found be stable and tunable emission properties, favoring them to use as eco-friendly fluorescent probes for sensing of wide variety target analytes.

A facile and novel Fe@SiO2-NH2@CDs-based chemosensor was fabricated for the identification of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) [58]. The fluorescence emission of CDs was quenched by the functionalization with Fe@SiO2-NH2, however, the fluorescence was remarkable restored upon the introduction of TNT. As a result, TNT was successfully replaced the CDs on the surface of Fe@SiO2-NH2, favoring to form a Meisenheimer complex between TNT (CH3 group) and NH2 group of Fe@SiO2–NH2. The developed chemosensor exhibited a wider linear range (10–2000 ng mL−1) with a LOD of 2.15 ng mL−1. The practical utility of developed chemosensor was successfully demonstrated for the detection of TNT in dust, soil and river water with good intra- and inter- day precisions. Further, it was noticed that Fe@SiO2–NH2 was found to be suitable to reuse for sensing of TNT, illustrating the developed magnetic CDs-based chemosensor could be used as miniaturized analytical tool for assaying of TNT in various samples. Recently, a facile and novel cytosensor was developed for the identification of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) with high selectivity and sensitivity using aptamers-decorated magnetic beads (MBs@Apt) and CDs/CoOOH nanosheets as a probe [22]. Authors functionalized magnetic beads with Apt and then CDs@FA were decorated onto the surfaces of cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets, yielding to form FA@CDs@CoOOH, which results to quench the fluorescence. In detection mechanism, MBs@Apt was identified and well accumulated in MCF-7 cells to from MBs@Apt@MCF-2 cells and then conjugated with FA@CDs@CoOOH to form sandwich nanostructures as MBs@Apt@MCF-2 cells/ FA@CDs@CoOOH. The assay of CTCs was based on the release of CDs as fluorescence signal due to the decomposition of CoOOH by ascorbic acid. Under the optimal condition, the developed method exhibited dynamic linear range (10 - 105 cells mL-1) with an impressive LOD of 5 cell/mL, signifying the promising analytical features of developed cytosensor for the detection of MCF-7 cells in white blood cells, which could be used as a rapid method for ultrasensitive detection of CTCs in clinical samples.

Recent years, the MNCDs combined with the surface imprinting tool have been successfully demonstrated for assaying of various target analytes because of their selectivity and sensitivity. For example, a facile and sensitive fluorescence method was developed for the detection of bovine hemoglobin using MIP@Fe3O4@CDs-polydopamine as a sensing platform [59]. In this approach, Fe3O4@CDs were fabricated by single-step hydrothermal approach and the surfaces of Fe3O4@CDs were functionalized with bovine hemoglobin via H-bonding. Subsequently, polydopamine was decorated on the surfaces of Fe3O4@CDs to immobilize protein using DA as a cross-liner and monomer. As a result, polydopamine has strong ability to interact with bovine hemoglobin via strong H-bonding and hydrophobic interactions. The fluorescence intensity was gradually decreased with increasing concentration of bovine hemoglobin (0.05–16.0 μM) with a LOD of 17.3 nM. The developed method exhibited good recovery ranges from 99.0–104.0%, indicating the developed probe could be useful for assaying bovine hemoglobin in various biofluids. Further, N-acyl homoserine lactones (AHLs) are considered as important intercellular signaling molecules, which play key role in efficiency sensing of microbial species (bacteria). In view of this, a facile analytical method was established for the detection of AHLs using CDs doped MIPs on the Fe3O4 NPs surfaces as a fluorescent probe [60]. The developed probe exhibited several features including fluorescence, rapid response, class-selectivity and superparamagnetism, favoring them to use as ultrasensitive probe for the detection of AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL) with a wider linear range from 3.65 × 10−3 - 0.96 × 10−1 μM. The detection phenomenon was based on the fluorescence quenching of MNCDs by AHLs. This method has proven to be a novel analytical tool for assaying of AHLs in various food samples (milk and fish juice). A fluorescent sensor was fabricated for the detection of TNP by combining three nanostructured materials i.e., magnetic covalent organic frameworks (MCOFs), MIPs and CDs, which results to form MCOFs@MIPs@CDs [61]. The developed sensor exhibited several analytical features including rapidity (<1.0 min), lower LOD (100 pM) and good recoveries (88.7%–103.4%), offering to use as rapid analytical tool for assaying of TNP in real samples.

Uranium is a hexavalent actinide that has been used as radioactive material for various nuclear applications. In view of this, it is highly needed to establish a dual probe for the removal and detection of U6+ ion in various environments. The magnetic nature carbon dots i.e., Fe3O4-carboxymethyl cellulose(CMC)@zeolite imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) @CDs [62] and polyethyleneimine(PEE)-CDs@MnFe2O4 [63] nanocomposites have been used as dual probe for the removal and detection of U6+ ion from various sample matrices. It was observed that Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8@CDs exhibited higher adsorption efficiency than that of Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8 for the removal of U6+ ion, revealing the combination of Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8 with CDs provides effective surfaces and nanostructures for the adsorption of U6+ ion with high degree [62]. Fig. 4 a illustrates the fabrication of Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8@CDs for the removal of U6+ ion. The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8@CDs was linearly quenched with increasing concentration of U6+ ion (0–100 mg L−1) (Fig. 4b). Noticeably, U6+ ion was effectively adsorbed onto the surfaces of magnetic CDs due to the presence of active functional groups on the surfaces of Fe3O4-CMC@ZIF-8@CDs. Similarly, a novel kind of PEE-CDs@MnFe2O4 nanocomposites was introduced for the removal of U6+ ion. Spectral data revealed that the high degree of U6+ ion adsorption was achieved due to the presence of active functional groups (COOH, NH 2 and OH) on the surface of MNCDs, offering effective adsorption of U6+ ion with high specificity [63]. To extend the applications of MNCDs, Fe3O4@carbon nanotubes (CNTs)@CDs nanocomposites were successfully fabricated for the removal of carbamazepine from water [64]. The removal mechanism was based on the strong π-π electron donor-acceptor interaction between Fe3O4@CNTs@CDs and carbamazepine.

A facile and selective fluorescence immunoassay was established for the detection of chloramphenicol using CDs-encapsulated CaCO3 and Fe3O4 NPs as a fluorescent immunoprobes [65]. Authors prepared the fluorescent immunoprobes by functionalizing the anti-chloramphenicol on the surfaces of CMC-CDs@CaCO3 nanospheres. Then, chitosan-Fe3O4 NPs were functionalized with chloroamphenicol hapten, felicitating immunoreaction by acting as a nano-carrier and interface. Similarly, Fe3O4 NPs were functionalized with undecanoic acid (UDA) and then conjugated with CDs to form Fe3O4 NPs-UDA@CDs for the detection and removal of benzo[a]pyrene from environmental water [66] (Fig. 5 a). It was noticed that Fe3O4 NPs-UDA@CDs exhibited higher adsorption capacity than that of Fe3O4 NPs-UDA nanocomposites, suggesting the modification of Fe3O4 NPs-UDA nanocomposites with CDs offers high adsorption abilities, which favors them to use as promising materials for removal of toxic chemicals. Magnetization curves demonstrated that the Fe3O4 NPs-UDA@CDs exhibited the superparamagnetic behavior and possessed strong magnetic nature when applied external magnetic field (Fig. 5b-c). Importantly, hydrophobic layer was noticed in Fe3O4 NPs-UDA@CDs, signifying the hydrophobic nature was changed due to the functionalization of Fe3O4 NPs-UDA with CDs, which allows them to well disperse in water. As a result, Fe3O4 NPs-UDA@CDs were efficiently adsorbed benzo[a]pyrene even in the presence of other chemical species. Compared to unmodified magnetic NPs, the functionalization of CDs on the surface of magnetic NPs ensures tremendous surface area and functional groups available for the MNCDs-ultratrace analyte interaction, which could improve the analytical performance of the probe with high selectivity even at extremely ultra-trace levels.

Recently, magnetic nature CDs were fabricated by either modifying the surfaces of magnetic nanocomposites or doping of magnetic elements. The MNCDs possess remarkable physico-chemical and optical properties with promising applications in sensing of inorganic species. To support this assumption, various inorganic species including Cu2+ [67], Hg2+ [68,69], Pb2+ [70], F− [71,72], Fe3+ and H2O2 [73] have been successfully identified by using magnetic nature CDs-based nanocomposites. Fe3O4@CMC@CDs were evolved as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ ion via fluorescence quenching mechanism [67], exhibiting a LOD of 0.56 μM, which could be used as a promising probe for Cu2+ ion sensing. Similarly, Fe3O4@CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal approach using ammonium ferric citrate and urea as precursors [68]. The fabricated Fe3O4@CDs exhibited dual nature i.e., detection and removal of Hg2+ ion, offering good linearity in the range of 2.0–200.0 nM with a LOD of 12.6 nM. This method was successfully applied to detect and to remove Hg2+ ion from water (sea and tap) and human serum samples. Another report also described the use of Fe3O4@CDs-based electrospinning nanofibers as potential adsorbents for the removal of Hg2+ ions [69]. The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@CDs nanofibers was linearly decreased with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ion, signifying the cumulative adsorption capacity of magnetic nanofibers towards Hg2+ ion. A simple and single-step solvothermal strategy was developed for the fabrication of MFe2O4 (M = Co, Mn)@MoS2@CDs nanohybrid composites for the removal of Pb2+ ion [70]. The prepared magnetic nanohybrid materials exhibited higher adsorption capacities (660.67 and 588.24 mg g−1 for CoFe2O4@MoS2@CDs and Mn Fe2O4@MoS2@CDs) and showed selective sorption behavior towards Pb2+ ion even in the presence of other competitive metal ions Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions. Importantly, the developed magnetic hybrid nanocomposites are found to be reusable for the removal of Pb2+ ion, demonstrating their green chemistry nature for water purification.

A novel amine-modified CDs-Mn+ (NCDs-Mn+) was fabricated and loaded on to the surfaces of mesoporous Fe3O4@SiO2 for the development of stable dual sensor (Fe3O4@mSiO2-SiCDs@diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)-Ni2+) for the detection of removal of F− ion [71]. In this work, authors prepared amine-modified CDs and the reversible binding between CDs and metal ions, yielding to create receptor for fluorescence quenching via nonradiative electron transfer from amino-modified CDs to metal ions. The created quenching receptor was loaded onto the Fe3O4@mSiO2 to form stable Fe3O4@mSiO2-SiCDs@DTPA-Mn+/amino CDs, which could be used as a sensor for sensing of F− ion. The developed probe exhibited several features such as lower LOD (65 nM), wider linear range (1.0–25 μM), reusability (6 times) and maximum removal capacity (21.4 mg/g). Another method also demonstrated the use of Fe3O4@SiO2@ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)@Ni2+ ion as fluorescent probe for the identification and removal of F− ion [72]. The detection mechanism was based on the ligand exchange between CDs and F− ion, exhibiting good linearity in the rang of 1–20 μM with an impressive LOD of 0.06 μM. Further, the developed probe was also demonstrated as imaging probe for the intracellular identification of F− ion.

Likewise, magnetic nature CDs were fabricated by doping Mn2+ ion into CDs via single-step hydrothermal approach using manganese tetraphenyl porphyrin and citric acid as reagents [3]. The fabricated Mn2+ ion-doped CDs exhibited emission peak at 482 nm and showed bright green fluorescence. Fe3+ ion was selectively detected by using Mn2+ ion-doped CDs as a sensor. The developed fluorescent magnetic sensor has ability to detect Fe3+ ion at 220 nM. A facile and sensitive fluorescence method was developed for the identification of H2O2, uric acid and glucose using the combination of B,N-doped CDs and ZnFe2O4 NPs [73]. It was observed that o-phenylenediamine was significantly produced yellow color (absorption maximum at 420 nm) by oxidation of phenylenediamine (oxOPD) due to the oxidizing nature of H2O2. The oxOPD was quenched the fluorescence of B,N-doped CDs) at 430 nm via the inner filter effect (IFE), favoring to produce a new emission peak at 556 nm. Based on this, quantitative analyses of H2O2, uric acid and glucose were carried out by measuring the intensity ratio at 556/430 nm, offering the detection limits of 0.09, 0.9 and 0.9 μM for H2O2, uric acid and glucose using colorimetry and of 0.1, 1.0 and 8.0 μM for H2O2, uric acid and glucose via ratiometric fluorescence assay, respectively. This method was successfully utilized to detect uric acid and glucose in biofluid (human serum).

Two independent studies have been successfully demonstrated the use of Mn2− ion-doped- and Fe3+ functionalized- CDs for the quantification of ascorbic acid in HeLa cells [74] and in rat brain microdialysates [75]. Mn2+ ion-doped CDs were prepared by single-step hydrothermal approach using tetrakis(hydroxymethyl)phosphonium chloride, sulfanilic acid, and MnCl2 as reagents (Fig. 6 ) [74]. The prepared Mn2+ ion-CDs exhibited excitation and emission wavelengths at 362 and 500 nm. Authors developed “fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing strategies for the detection of Mn7+ ion and ascorbic acid. The emission peak of Mn2+ ion-doped CDs was quenched by Mn7+ ion via IFE through the effective coordination bonds between Mn2+ ion-doped CDs and Mn7+ ion as well as the redox reaction between OH groups of Mn2+ ion-doped CDs and Mn7+ ion. However, the quenched mission peak of Mn2+ ion-doped CDs@Mn7+ ion conjugates was effectively restored with the addition of ascorbic acid via redox reaction between ascorbic acid and Mn7+ ion. Under the identical conditions, this method exhibited good linear responses in the range of 3–150 and of 3–140 μM for Mn7+ ion and ascorbic acid, respectively. The developed probe has shown ability to detect Mn7+ ion and ascorbic acid even at 0.66 and 0.90 μM, respectively. Similarly, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Fe3+ ion and restored by the addition of ascorbic acid, favoring to establish fluorescence “on-off-on” analytical method via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which offers an impressive detection limit of 9.1 nM for ascorbic acid assay. The developed probe was successfully demonstrated for the detection of ascorbic acid in biofluids (rat brain microdialysates). A facile, selective and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence method was demonstrated for simultaneous detection of cholesterol and uric acid in human serum using N- and Co2+ ion-doped CDs with 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) as a probe via IFE process [28]. Authors fabricated N- and Co2+ ion-doped CDs via hydrothermal approach using diethylenetriamine, citric acid, and CoCl2 as precursor. DAP was formed due to the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by H2O2. The as-fabricated N,Co2+ ion-doped CDs exhibited ferromagnetic behavior and good optical properties, allowing them to use as fluorescent sensor for the detection of cholesterol and uric acid with LODs of 3.6 and 3.4 nM for cholesterol and uric acid, respectively. This assay was successfully demonstrated for assaying of uric acid and cholesterol in biofluids. Another simple hydrothermal method was adopted for the fabrication of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs using Gd(NO3)3 and 3-aminothiophenol as precursors [76]. A ratiometric fluorescence assay was developed for the detection of morin using Gd3+ ion-doped CDs as a nanosensor. Interestingly, a simple colorimetric assay was also established for the detection of morin by noticing color change of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs from colorless to deep-yellow. The emission intensity of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs was greatly enhanced with the addition of morin via the formation of complex between active functional groups (OH and CO) of morin and Gd3+ ion-doped CDs. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs at 490 nm was progressively increased with increasing concentration of morin (0∼150 μM) while the intensity at 415 nm was gradually decreased, demonstrating Gd3+ ion-doped CDs could be used as a ratiometric fluorescence “turn-on” nanosensor for the detection of morin, which showed a good linearity in the range of 0.04∼80.0 μM with a LOD of 25.0 nM. Similarly, the absorption peaks of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs at 280 and 420 nm were blue-sfhited to 262 and 415 nm with increasing concentration of morin (0∼112.0 μM), exhibiting good linear ranges of 0.04–15.0 and 15.0–93.0 μM, with detection limits of 10.5 and 21.2 nM, respectively. The developed assays exhibited good recoveries in the range of 97.63–105.01% in urine samples, illustrating the feasibility of the method for assaying of morin in real samples.

Further, Fe3O4@CDs were decorated onto the surfaces of polyacrylonitrile nanofibers for the detection of famotidine by resonance light scattering (RLS) method [77]. Fe3O4@CDs@ polyacrylonitrile nanofibers exhibited light scattering properties with and without addition of famotidine, favoring to establish a quantitative assay of famotidine with wider linear range (0.15–50.0 μM), with a LOD of 0.04 μM. This method exhibited good recoveries in the range of 98.77–103.05 %, and successfully demonstrated to assay famotidine in pharmaceutical and human serum samples. Another approach was described the use of Fe3O4 NPs as adsorbents for SPE of ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin from milk and then dispersed in CDs solution for the fluorescence detection of both drugs [78]. The fluorescence quantitative assays of ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin were based on the enhancement of fluorescence emission peak at 412 nm with increasing amounts of ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin, respectively. This method exhibited good linear in the range of 0.02–1.0 and 0.02–1.25 of μM for ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin, with LODs of 6.7 and 4.6 nM for ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin, respectively.

A facile fluorescence assay of glyphosate was carried out using IgG antibodies-CDs and glyphosate-Fe3O4 NPs via immune reaction [79]. In this approach, antigen-Fe3O4-glyphosate was added into the antibodies-IgG-CDs, resulting to separate antigen- Fe3O4 NPs via immune reaction, which could be used as a signal for the quantification of glyphosate. As a result, this method exhibited a good linearity (0.01–80 μg/mL) with a LOD of 8 ng/mL. Importantly, this method showed high selectivity to detect glyphosate and has ability to the specific identification of glyphosate and its distributions in plant tissues, which could be visualized with naked-eye. A simple fluorescence method was developed for the detection of ochratoxin A using Fe3+ ion -doped porous carbon and aptamer-functionalized N-doped GQDs as a sensor [80]. The assay of ochratoxin A was based on the resumption of emission peak of aptamer-N-doped GQDs -Apt) as probes. Since Fe3+ ion-doped porous carbon was quenched the fluorescence of aptamer-N-doped GQDs, however, it was restored with the introduction of ochratoxin A. Further, exonulease I was digested the aptamer, favoring to release ochratoxin A and to interact with another Fe3+ ion -doped porous carbon and aptamer-functionalized N-doped GQDs. A simple and portable analytical strategy was described that exhibits good linearity in the range of 10–5000 nM, with a LOD of 2.28 nM. Another fluorescence method was developed for the detection of TNP using Fe3O4@MIP@CDs as a sensor [81]. This method exhibited high sensitivity for the assay of TNP even at 0.5 nM, and successfully applied to quantify TNP in water samples with good precision and accuracy.

Bovine β-lactoglobulin has been identified as one of the key allergenic protein in milk (cow), causing allergic reaction even at ultratrace levels. To address this issue, a simple fluorescence assay of β-lactoglobulin was carried out by using CDs as an indicator [82]. In this work, Fe3O4@aptamer@c-DNA@CDs were prepared and used for the assay of β-lactoglobulin via the hybridization between cDNA and aptamer. The fluorescence intensity of c-DNA@CDs at 447 nm was progressively increased with increasing amounts of β-lactoglobulin, showing a good linear range (0.25–50 ng mL−1), with a LOD of 37 pg mL−1. This method was efficiently illustrated to detect β-lactoglobulin in various food samples, which signifies that the promising applications of the method in food industries. Another study illustrated the use of CDs@DNA@Fe3O4@polydopamine (PDA) nanocomposites for the fluorescence assay of microRNA-167 via FRET mechanism [83]. In this approach, CDs@DNA acted as a donor and Fe3O4@PDA acted as an acceptor, resulting to establish FRET mechanism. Noticeably, Ca2+ ion was significantly improved the absorption ability Fe3O4@PDA NPs, causing to quench the emission peak of CDs at 445 nm (Fig. 7 a). Upon the addition of microRNA-167, CDs-pDNA-microRNA-167 conjugates will be separated from the Fe3O4@PDA NPs surfaces due to week π interactions, which favors to restore the fluorescence of CDs. As a result, a calibration graph was established between fluorescence intensity and concentration of microRNA-167, showing a good linearity in the range of 0.5–100 nM, which has ability to sense microRNA-167 even at 76 pM. The developed biosensor was explored to quantify microRNA-167 in the entire microRNA extractions from A. thaliana seedlings.

The identification and quantification of pathogenic bacterial species play an essential role in various sectors including food industries, environmental, water and biological sciences. The sensing applications of magnetic nature carbon dots have been successfully extended for the detection of various bacterial species Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, respectively [84–86]. Briefly, Fe3O4@CDs@chitosan was used as a biosensor for the identification of two bacterial species (S. aureus and E. coli), offering the LODs of 3 × 102 and 3.5 × 102 cfu mL−1 for S. aureus and E. coli, respectively [84,85]. Another study demonstrated the use of NiFe2O4@CDs for the identification of Pseudomonas aeruginosa [86]. The as-prepared NiFe2O4@CDs showed ferromagnetic behavior, and successfully used as a sensor for sensing of multiple analytes Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions via fluorescence enhancement. These reports illustrated that the magnetic carbon dots were efficiently used as bacterial sensor.

A series of MnO2@CDs nanocomposites-based analytical methods have been successfully demonstrated for assaying of various target analytes including As3+ ion [87], H2O2 [88,89], ascorbic acid [90,91], alkaline phosphatase [92], biothiols (6-thioguanine (6-TG) and 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) [93], glutathione (GSH) [94], organophosphorus pesticides [95] and aflatoxin B1 [96] with remarkable selectivity and sensitivity. The MnO2@CDs acted as As3+ ion sensor since the addition of As3+ ion induces the decomposition of MnO2 and terminates FRET process, resulting the restore of emission intensity of CDs [87]. The MnO2@CDs-based fluorescence method exhibited a good linear graph between the concentration of As3+ ion (0–200 nM) and fluorescence intensity, which achieved a LOD of 16.8 nM (1.40 ppb). This method was applied to detect As3+ ion in various water samples. Further, MnO2@CDs-based fluorescence ‘turn-on’ assay was developed for the detection of H2O2 in sea food [88]. The MnO2@CDs were formed via FRET, which results to quench the fluorescence of CDs. With the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence intensity was progressively increased due to the redox reaction between H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheets. As a result, a simple analytical strategy was developed for the sensing of H2O2 with an impressive LOD of 0.87 μM. Another analytical strategy was described the use of Fe3O4@MnO2@N-doped CDs as dual sensor (electrochemical and fluorescence) for the detection of H2O2 [89]. Authors noticed that Fe3O4@MnO2 nanocomposites acted as electrochemical enhancer and fluorescence quencher. It was observed that Fe3O4@MnO2 was quenched the emission peak of CDs and catalyzed ferrocene oxidation reaction in the absence of H2O2 whereas the fluorescence of N-doped CDs was recovered due to the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ ion, which yields to decrease the oxidation signal of ferrocene. This method was achieved good detection limits of 0.6 and 1.0 μM for electrochemical and fluorescent sensors.

Two parallel studies illustrated the use of MnO2@CDs-based fluorescence analytical approaches for the detection of ascorbic acid in various real samples including vegetables, fresh fruits, and fruit juices [90,91]. It was noticed that MnO2@CDs exhibited dual sensor for the detection of ascorbic acid by fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [90]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the weak fluorescence was noticed in the MnO2@CDs solution without ascorbic acid, however, the fluorescence intensity of was progressively increased with increasing concentrations of ascorbic acid (0–80 μM) (Fig. 8a), which is due to the reducing ability of ascorbic acid to reduce MnO2 nanosheet to Mn2+ ion. Based on this, MnO2@CDs acted as MRI contrast probes for the sensing of ascorbic acid. The influence of ascorbic acid on spin-lattice (T1) and spin-spin (T2) relaxation time of MnO2@CDs was examined to study the analytical performance for assay of ascorbic acid using MR signal (Fig. 8c and e). It was seen that T1 and T2 times were drastically decreased with increasing amounts of ascorbic acid (0–80 μM) due to the increase of Mn2+ ion, favoring to construct calibration graphs between longitudinal relaxation rate (R1 = 1/T1) and transverse relaxation rate (R2 = 1/T2) of MnO2@CDs and concentration of ascorbic acid (0–80 μM) (Fig. 8b and d), demonstrating the MnO2@CDs could be used as a probe for the quantification of ascorbic acid in the MR mode. The MnO2@CDs-based fluorescence assay of ascorbic acid was based on the IFE strategy [90,91]. Both the studied demonstrated that the emission intensity of CDs was decreased by functionalizing CDs on the surfaces of MnO2 via IFE, however ascorbic acid was efficiently restored the fluorescence intensity of CDs due to the redox reaction between MnO2 and ascorbic acid. As a result, the constructed calibration graphs exhibited good linear ranges of 0–80 μM and of 0.18–90 μM for the detection of ascorbic acid with detection limits of 2.89 μM [90] and 42 nM [91]. The developed strategies showed good selectivity towards ascorbic acid sensing, and successfully applied to quantify ascorbic acid in various samples. Noticeably, MnO2@CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and selectivity towards ascorbic acid, offering them to use as promising probe for in vivo imaging of AA in mice [90].

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was recognized as a key essential hydrolases that is highly reactive in various biochemical pathways, which also acted as a key biomarker for various diseases such as bone disease, breast and prostatic cancer, diabetes and liver dysfunction [97]. In view of this, MnO2@CDs-based analytical strategy was developed for the detection of ALP via FRET process [92]. Under the optimal conditions, the emission intensity of CDs at 420 nm was gradually increased with increasing amounts of ALP, achieving a lower LOD of 0.4 U/L. The combination of CDs and MnO2 nanosheet has been recognized as promising sensor for the detection of anticancer drugs (6-TG and 6-MP) [93] and glutathione [94]. The fluorescence assays of thiol containing molecules are based on FRET mechanism, where MnO2 acts as acceptor and CDs act as electron donor. In both methods, the quenching behavior of MnO2 was completely masked due to the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ ion via the formation of Mn-S bond. The developed MnO2@CDs-based FRET sensors exhibited LODs of 0.014, 0.015 and 0.3 μM for 6-MP, 6 TG and glutathione, respectively. The developed MnO2@CDs-based fluorescent sensors were successfully applied to assay of 6-MP, 6 TG in pharmaceutical samples and of glutathione in biofluids.

To extend on-site applications of MnO2@CDs nanocomposites, acetylthiocholine (ATCh)@butyrylcholinesterase(BChE)@MnO2@CDs-based fluorescence method was developed for the detection of paraoxon in various samples agricultural samples (water (river and tap), cabbage and rice) [95]. The detection phenomenon was based on FRET process between CDs and MnO2 nanosheets. Authors noticed that MnO2 nanosheets were greatly decomposed via enzymatic reaction (thiocholine) in the presence of BChE and ATCh, yielding to recover the fluorescence of CDs. However, paraoxon was efficiently inhibited the activity of BChE, preventing the production of thiocholine and reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ ion, which allows to establish a quantitative method for assay of paraoxon via fluorescence “turn-off” process. Authors noticed that a significant color change with varying concetration of target analytes, demonstrating visual read-out assay for on-site identification of OPs. Further, the developed sensor was successfully detected paraoxon in the presence of other similar pesticides, illustrating the selectivity of the probe towards paraoxon. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was successfully quantified in foodstuff by visual and photoelectrochemical immunosensing approaches using CDs@MnO2 as a probe [96]. In this approach, immunoreaction was initiated on anti-AFB1 antibody-coated Fe3O4 beads by using AFB1 conjugates (glucose oxidase (GOx)-AFB1-bovine serum albumin, favoring to form immunocomplexes. Briefly, H2O2 was generated by the oxidative reaction of GOx, thereby yielding to reduce/etch MnO2 to Mn2+ ion, which allows to separate CDs from the surface of electrode. As a result, the photocurrent of MnO2@CDs-decorated electrode was decreased with increasing amount of H2O2, and subsequently the color of MnO2@CDs-decorated electrode will be changed, favoring to establish visual and photoelectrochemical immunosensing platfoms for the dection of AFB1. The developed sensor showed dynamic linear range from 0.01–20 ng mL–1, which offers to detect AFB1 even at 2.1 pg mL–1 (ppt). The developed method showed good precision and accuracy for the detection of AFB1 in real samples. These studies revealed that the modification of magnetic nanostructures with CDs offers outstanding sensing applications for the quantification of wide variety molecules from complex samples. Table 2 summarizes the sensing applications of MNCDs for the detection of various target analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic nature carbon dots in bioimaging and magnetic resonance imaging
ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous deepening of researches on the bioimaging applications of CDs, the surfaces of CDs have been functionalized with different organic functional groups and used them as surface ligands for the functionalization of various nanocomposites including magnetic nanomaterials, which allows them to use in bioimaging and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging applications [98–100], S32-S34]. In terms of bioimaging and MR imaging, the magnetic carbon dots have shown extraordinary performances to visualize the cells with high specificity and resolution [11]. Importantly, they provides simple methodologies for imaging of cells and tumors in various tissues and samples. In recent years, the MR imaging of cells and tissues has found to be an essential tool for the contrast imaging and diagnostics [S35-S44]. The MNCDs have been recognized as promising candidates for bioimaging/MR imaging of various target cells due to their unique properties including high aqueous solubility, magnetic nature, strong PL, stable PL, and superior resistance to photobleaching. Several research paper demonstrated that the MNCDs have been acted as promising biocompatible fluorescent probes for biosensing and bioimaging applications in biomedical sciences [S43-S51]. With these outstanding properties of MNCDs, several research groups have been used as MNCDs as imaging probes for bioimaging and MR imaging of various cells and tissues. The magnetic nature CDs were generated by either doping of metal ions with magnetic nature or the decorating of CDs on the surfaces of magnetic nature nanocomposites.

For example, gadopentetic acid-tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris base) and betaine were used as reagents for the fabrication of Ga -doped via pyrolysis method [25]. Similarly, a novel multifunctional probe was fabricated by assembling Gd3+ ion onto the surface of chlorine e6-CDs [21]. The developed probe was efficiently used for the investigation of antitumor effect in mice, which is having A549 tumor. A novel magnetic nature CDs-based ratiometric sensor was fabricated by the combination of Fe3O4@dual-emissive CDs@MOFs [S36]. The fabricated probe exhibited several advantages including high adsorption, selectivity, separation, and ratiometric sensing ability. The Fe3O4@dual-emissive CDs@MOFs-based fluorescence method was demonstrated for the sensing of HClO. Further, Gd3+ ion-doped CDs were efficiently used as promising probes for MR imaging guided radiotherapy of cancer tissues [S37]. The as prepared Gd3+ ion-doped CDs (∼18 nm) are well dispersed in aqueous media, and showed a strong photoluminescence behavior. These features make them as good candidates for accumulation into kidney and for radiation therapy. In this approach, Gd3+ ion-doped CDs were prepared by using gadopentetic acid and glycine as precursors (Fig. 9 a). The cytotoxicity studies demonstrated that Gd2+ ion-doped CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity in human lung adenocarcinoma HepG2 cells with good cell viability (∼90 %). Noticeably, Gd3+ ion-doped CDs showed good bright-field images without any morphological changes, signifying their biocompatibility and fluorescence nature (Fig. 9b). It clearly seen that Gd3+ ion-doped CDs were efficiently internalized into the cells and reached to the cytoplasm in a very short time. Further, MR contrast nature of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs was studied in in vitro alongside using Magnevist as a control (commercial contrast agent) (Fig. 9c), demonstrating that r 1 value of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs showed high value (6.45 mM−1s−1) than that of Magnevist (4.05 M−1s−1). Fig. 10 a shows that the images of mice by intravenously injecting Gd3+ ion-doped CDs into the mice, and studied T1-weighted images at different time intervals (0–6 h). It was also noticed that Gd3+ ion-doped CDs exhibited a longer circulation time as compared to Magnevist (Fig. 10b), possessing remarkable renal clearance, and excreted from the organ through urine. These images revealed that Gd3+ ion-doped CDs acted as promising contrast agent and effectively enhanced T1 relaxations in various organs (heart, bladder and kidney) in vivo. Similar studies were also studied by replacing Gd3+ ion with zerovalent iron as a dopant into CDs [S38]. The zerovalent iron-doped CDs exhibited good MR imaging and fluorescence imaging properties for in vivo MR angiogram and the liver targeted MRI, illustrating their multimodal imaging nature with high specificity.

A series of studies has been demonstrated the use of metal ions doped (Gd3+ and Mn2+ ions) CDs for fluorescence imaging and MR imaging of various cells and tissues [S39-S51]. To examine their contrasting agent properties, Gd3+ or Mn2+ ions-doped CDs were intravenously injected into target rumors bearing either mice or mouse. The measured T1-weighted images demonstrated that T1 signals in tumors were amazingly increased, signifying their novel property as contrasting agents for the target tumor studies. The Gd3+ ion-doped CDs@Fe3O4 was fabricated for dual modal imaging (fluorescence and MR imaging) studies [S39]. A dual emissive (green and red) nanoprobe was designed by combining polydopamine@Mn2+ ion/N-doped CDs [S40]. The fluorescence imaging applications of polydopamine@Mn2+ ion/N-doped CDs were evaluated on A549 and HeLa cells by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CSLM). The fluorescence images demonstrated that luminescence was generated due to the internalization of polydopamine@Mn2+ ion/N-doped CDs into the cells. Ultra-small Mn2+ ion-doped CDs were used for in vivo MR and ex vivo imaging of mouse brain [S44], offering an efficient platform for the identification and location of tiny brain gliomas in mouse brain. Several studies have been illustrated the use of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs as potential agents for fluorescence and MR imaging of wide variety cells including HeLa, A549, NCI-H446 cells and human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs), respectively. These studies demonstrated that Gd3+ ion-doped CDs exhibited stable fluorescence in the presence of several aminoacids, revealing the potentiality of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs for imaging of cells in vivo studies. The biocompatible nature of Gd3+ ion-doped CDs provides efficient conditions to monitor the tumor size and growth, which helps to identify the stage of cancer disease. The Gd3+ ion-doped CDs also exhibited higher cellular uptake ability and accumulated in the cytoplasm, showing strong multiple fluorescence color, which signifies their multi-functionality in bioanalytical sciences. Overall, the integration of MNCDs with fluorescence and MR imaging techniques has been found to be promising biotools for the visualization and location of tumor cells and for monitoring of the tumor growth, offering several features than that of other imaging tools.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theranostic applications of magnetic nature carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, magnetic nature carbon nanostructures have been recognized as promising theranostic agents for the treatment of various tumors [12]. Magnetic nature CDs have shown remarkable advantages over other magnetic nanocomposites because of their acceptable optical, physico-chemical and magnetic properties. As a result, magnetic nature CDs nanostructures have also been successfully applied for the diagnostic and therapeutic applications [S44, S52-S57]. In this section, the progress on the magnetic nature carbon nanostructures in theranostics applications is summarized. Briefly, Gd3+ ion-doped CDs have been explored as a novel theranostic nanoplatform for MRI-guided radiotherapy of tumors [S37]. It was noticed that Gd3+ ion-doped CDs were effectively accumulated in the kidney via enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect, and showed remarkable radiosensitization enhancements and longitudinal relaxivity rate (r1) (6.45 mM−1S−1), which help to clear out bladder from the body. Similarly, Gd2O3@GQD nanocomposites have been used as MRI and fluorescent sensors for biomedical applications [S52]. Noticeably, the longitudinal relaxivity (r1 = 15.995 mM−1 s−1) was greatly improved using Gd2O3@GQD nanocomposites as compared commercial Magnevist (r1 = 4.5 mM−1 s−1), which makes them as potential MR T1 contrast agent. The penetrable ability of Gd2O3 was greatly improved by modifying the surface of Gd2O3 with GQD. Further, the biocompatibility of Gd2O3@GQD nanocomposites was investigated by MTT assay on MCF-7 cancer cells, showing good cell viability when Gd concentration at 60 μM, which confirms the Gd2O3@GQD nanocomposites exhibited good biocompatibility as compared to other contrast agents (Fe3O4 and Gd2O3 based nanocomposites).

Magnetofluorescent CDs were successfully fabricated by doping Mn2+ ion into CDs nanostructures [S53]. It was noticed that Mn2+ ion-doped CDs acted as a smart contrast probe for MR T1 imaging (relaxivity value of 6.97 mM−1 s−1) and near-infrared (NIR) emission peak at 745 nm. Importantly, Mn2+ ion-doped CDs showed high ability to catalize hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate oxygen, offering to use as good candidates for oxygen generation in hypoxic solid tumors, which could be used for bimodal fluorescence/MR imaging as well as photodynamic therapy (PDT) of tumors. Dehvari’s group developed a facile synthetic route for the fabrication of multifunctional MNCDs by doping various heteroatoms including S, N and Mn2+ ion [S54]. The multifunctional MNCDs exhibited several features such as high QY (17.7 %), scavenging activity against reactive oxygen species and reactive nitrogen species (RNS). To extend their theranostic applications, multifunctional MNCDs were functionalized with hyaluronic acid (HA) for in vivo studies on zebrafish, showing high ability to confirm the 44 (CD44)-overexpressing B16F1 cells, which was further confirmed by in vitro studies by confocal fluorescence microscopy and MRI. Noticeably, the multifunctional MNCDs@HA@doxorubicin showed a remarkable opportunity in theranostics especially realizing of doxorubicin, thereby exhibiting greater cytotoxicity (90 %) to toward B16F1 cancer cells as compared to free doxorubicin treatment (60 %). when compared to free DOX treatment group (60 %). Further, amphiphilic lipopeptide assemblies and iron(II) phthalocyanine were used as precursors for the preparation of magnetic nature liposomal CDs hybrid nanostructures [S55]. The as-prepared magnetic nature liposomal CDs showed several features such as high photothermal conversion capability (η = 63.4 %) and gene transfection efficiency in 4T1 tumor cell (3-fold increase) and in animal model (2-fold increase). Noticeably, magnetic nature liposomal CDs exhibited high ability to inhibit tomor growth (80 %) in a breast tumor tissues. Another report described that the preparation of Mn2+ ion-doped CDs via microwave-assisted method using urea, citric acid, and MnCl2 as precursors [S44]. It was observed that Mn2+ ion-doped CDs provides good resolution MR images for the investigation of tumors in the tiny brain glioma region, illustrating Mn2+ ion-doped CDs were successfully entered into the brain by crossing through blood-brain barrier (BBB), facilitating to intact/disrupt BBB and to enter into the tumor vasculature of gliomas through EPR effect. The cytotoxicity of Mn2+ ion-doped CDs was investigated by MTT assay C6 cells, showing good survival rate of c6 cells with a satisfactory cell viability (>80 %). As a result, Mn2+ ion-doped CDs acted as promising probes for the sensing of tiny brain gliomas. Similarly, MnOx doped CDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal approach using manganese(III) acetylacetonate as a precursor [S56]. The as-prepared MnOx-CDs exhibit several features such as good biocompatibility, water dispersibility, and optical properties (326/442 nm excitation/emission wavelengths and QY of 11.3 %), favoring them as potential candidates for cell imaging. A new magnetic carbon hybrid nanostructure was prepared by functionalizing single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) with PEG 2000N@ Fe3O4@CDs for the treatment of cancer [S57]. It was noticed that SWNTs@PEG-2000N@Fe3O4@CDs@doxorubicin was efficiently inhibited the growth of tumor and effectively accumulated in the malignant tumor issues, demonstrating their potentiality for cervical cancer treatment. These studies revealed that the magnetic nature CDs nanostructures have proven to be promising candidates for theranostic applications.

Since the first synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots in 2004, extensive research work has been carried out on the synthesis and applications of fluorescent carbon dots. To witness the magnetic nature of carbon dots, we have attempted to give a general review of the recent advances in fabricating magneto fluorescent carbon dots, and their applications in analytical chemistry from separation science to sensing and bioimaging/MR imaging. Recent years, doping of magnetic nature metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+ and Gd3+ ions) into CDs nanostructures and fabrication of magnetic CDs hybrid nanocomposites is greatly improved the physico-chemical and magnetic properties of CDs, favoring to extend their applications in environmental analytical chemistry and biomedical sciences. In this review, the synthesis and properties of magnetic nature CDs nanostructures were briefly described. It was noticed that the CDs exhibited magnetic nature with acceptable optical properties, thereby offering them to use in multiple applications (fluorescence, extraction, adsorption, and biomedical). The magnetic nature CDs nanocomposites were successfully used as potential candidates for the separation of wide variety chemical compounds with minimized sample preparation. Further, the MNCDs exhibited good PL nature for the detection of inorganic, organic and biomolecules in environmental and biological samples, offering remarkable advantages such as selectivity, sensitivity, rapidity and effective extraction as compared to nonmagnetic CDs and other nanomaterials. As a result, the MNCDs have evolved as the emerging new class of nanomaterials in separation science and sensing/bio- and MR- imaging due to their facile synthetic routes, high dispersability in aqueous media, remarkable optical properties (excitation-dependent multi-color emission, and high photostability) and biocompatibility. The magnetic nature was created due to either doping of magnetic nature metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+ and Gd3+ ions) or functionalization of CDs on the surfaces of magnetic nature metal oxide nanocomposites, favoring them to act as promising sorbents for the extraction/separation and identification of wide variety chemical species from complex samples and to act as efficient sensor for the selective identification of ultra-trace target analytes with lower LODs.

The MNCDs fabricated by various approaches (hydrothermal, sonochemical, pyrolysis, microwave and co-precipitation), thereby providing unique surface functionalities, which offers excellent magnetic and optical properties. As a result, the MNCDs have shown outstanding performances for the separation of trace level target analytes from environmental and biofluids. The as-synthesized MNCDs exhibited several features such as magnetic nature (ferromagnetic, paramagnetic and superparamagnetic), good fluorescence properties with acceptable QYs and biocompatibility, exploring them as good candidates for sensing of ultra-trace target analytes at low volume of samples, which could resolve the issues (interference from other chemical species, expensive instrumentation, large volume of samples) in the analytical chemistry. The spectroscopic and electron microscopic data demonstrated that the insertion of magnetic nature metal ions and metal oxide nanocomposites into CDs, the CDs exhibited good magnetic resonance effect and used as promising agents for MR imaging in vitro and in vivo.

Spectral data revealed that the biocompatibility, stability, dispersability, QY, bright fluorescence, magnetic nature of CDs are highly dependent on the metal ion doping concentration, surface ligands, and pH. The MNCDs-based fluorescent sensors requires the immobilization of enzyme/protein to perform immunoreactions for assaying of various target analytes via redox reactions. The utility of MNCDs in imaging of cells and in identifying tumors locations in various organs is of great interest because of their unique properties. Although these reports have described the innovative synthetic strategies for the fabrication of MNCDs and their applications in analytical chemistry from separation science to detection and imaging of target species, but some of the challenges still need to be addressed (i) user and eco- friendly synthetic approaches should develop for the fabrication of MNCDs, which could minimize multiple steps for nanoarchitecture on the surfaces of MNCDs, (ii) mostly metal ions-doped MNCDs have been applied as probes for fluorescence sensing/imaging applications, significant efforts must be needed to extend their application to various branches of analytical and environmental chemistry, (iii) similarly, the maximum number of studies has been investigated on the use of three metal ions (Gd3+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and zerovalent iron) doped CDs for fluorescence/MR imaging of selected cells (HeLa, A549, NCI-H446 and hMSCs), the fluorescence/MR imaging studies should be carried out on other cells/tissue, which endow the practical applications of MNCDs in biomedical sciences, (iv) biodegradability of MNCDs after using them in separation science is key feature that requires detailed investigations to define the non-toxic nature of MNCDs and (v) the assessment on life cycle and reusability studies need to be studied on magnetic nature CDs nanocomposites, which will tune their use in analytical chemistry from laboratory to commercial applications. Finally, the on-going research work on the MNCDs in analytical chemistry (separation to detection and fluorescence/MR imaging) is offering analytical features and providing promising analytical platforms, and it is expecting to extend their applications in the development of miniaturized optical tools in analytical and biomedical sciences.
